response:
A night-shift network engineer monitors an organization's infrastructure, which consists of a series of interconnected servers. The reliability of each server $ S_i $ in the network can be modeled by a continuous random variable $ R_i $ following a normal distribution $ N(\mu_i, \sigma_i^2) $. The engineer also collaborates with the cybersecurity team to mitigate potential threats, which can be modeled as a Poisson process with a rate $ \lambda $ of attacks per hour.

<<vegetable>> The probability that at least 9 out of the 10 servers will have a reliability of at least 0.95 at any given time can be calculated using the binomial distribution. Each server has a probability of $ P(R_i \geq 0.95) = 1 - P(R_i < 0.95) $. Using the normal distribution, $ P(R_i < 0.95) $ can be calculated using the Z-score formula $ Z = \frac{0.95 - \mu_i}{\sigma_i} $. For $ \mu_i = 0.98 $ and $ \sigma_i = 0.02 $, the Z-score is $ Z = \frac{0.95 - 0.98}{0.02} = -1.5 $. Using the standard normal distribution table, $ P(Z < -1.5) \approx 0.0668 $. Thus, $ P(R_i \geq 0.95) \approx 1 - 0.0668 = 0.9332 $. The probability that at least 9 out of 10 servers have a reliability of at least 0.95 is then calculated using the binomial probability formula: $ P(X \geq 9) = \sum_{k=9}^{10} \binom{10}{k} (0.9332)^k (0.0668)^{10-k} $. This results in a probability of approximately 0.0012.

<<vegetable>> If the cybersecurity team detects an average of 3 attacks per hour, the probability that exactly 5 attacks will be detected during a 2-hour monitoring period can be calculated using the Poisson distribution. The Poisson probability formula is $ P(X = k) = \frac{\lambda^k e^{-\lambda}}{k!} $.
